title: The First Sunday in Lent
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
March 11, 2019 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The First Sunday in Lent, following Ash Wednesday (March 6, 2019). The Revised Common Lectionary readings would have included Genesis 2:15-17, 3:1-7 (the temptation of Adam and Eve), Psalm 32, Romans 5:12-19 (Paul on sin and grace through Christ), and Matthew 4:1-11 (Jesus's temptation in the wilderness). The sermon falls at the beginning of the forty-day Lenten season, traditionally emphasizing repentance, spiritual discipline, and preparation for Easter (April 21, 2019).

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the period following the 2018 General Convention (held in Austin, Texas in July 2018), which had approved allowing same-sex marriage rites and made other progressive resolutions. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert Wright (elected 2015), would have been engaged in normal Lenten programming. No major diocesan controversies or bishop elections were occurring at this specific time, though the national church continued processing the implications of 2018 Convention decisions.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical early spring weather with snow still possible at higher elevations. The city was focused on local politics ahead of municipal elections scheduled for May 2019. The Colorado legislature was in session (January-May 2019), debating various bills including education funding and healthcare measures. No major weather disasters or immediate community crises dominated Denver news in the week before March 11.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was facing ongoing investigations, with the Mueller Report (examining Russian interference in 2016 elections) expected to be released within weeks (delivered March 22, 2019). College admissions scandal "Operation Varsity Blues" was breaking news, with arrests of wealthy parents and celebrities announced on March 12, 2019—just one day after this sermon. The #MeToo movement continued to shape cultural conversation, with ongoing workplace harassment discussions.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Venezuela was experiencing severe political and humanitarian crisis under Nicolas Maduro, with opposition leader Juan Guaidó recognized by the US and attempting to challenge Maduro's government. The UK was in the midst of Brexit chaos, with Parliament repeatedly rejecting Prime Minister Theresa May's withdrawal agreement (the third "meaningful vote" occurred March 29, 2019). The Christchurch mosque shootings in New Zealand had occurred just days earlier (March 15, 2019—four days after this sermon), shocking the world.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The zeitgeist was one of political polarization and institutional strain, with Americans deeply divided on multiple fronts. The Marvel Cinematic Universe was in peak cultural moment (Avengers: Endgame would release April 26, 2019), offering escapism. Lent 2019 arrived during a period when American churches across denominations were grappling with cultural fragmentation, declining membership, and questions about institutional credibility. Many congregations were using Lenten reflection to address social divisions and spiritual renewal amid national turbulence.
